response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As someone who's spent years dealing with the pressures of cleaning jobs and managing a household, I've come to realize that social media can be a double-edged sword. On one hand, it's a great way to connect with friends and family who live far away, and I love scrolling through my newsfeed to see what's going on in the world. However, as someone who's prone to depression, I've found that excessive social media use can be really damaging. I've seen so many fake accounts and stories that are just meant to spread misinformation and stress people out. It's like, why bother? Can't we just focus on the good things in life and leave the drama behind? I've tried to limit my social media use to just a few hours a day, and I've also started to fact-check everything I read on there. It's not always easy, but it's worth it to me to stay sane and keep my mental health in check. I mean, who needs all that extra stress in their life, right? I've even started to avoid some of the platforms altogether, like Facebook, because it just seems like too much of a hassle. Twitter's better for me, because it's more straightforward and less likely to have fake content. But even there, I try to stick to accounts that I know and trust. I guess what I'm saying is that it's all about balance and caution when it comes to social media. We need to be smart and critical thinkers, and not just swallow everything we see without question. That's my two cents, anyway!
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I've come to realize that excessive social media use can be damaging to my mental health, so I've tried to limit my use and fact-check everything I read. I've also started to avoid some platforms altogether and stick to accounts that I know and trust.
